\section{Root of Equation e^x (x - 1) = e^-x (x + 1)}
Tags: Analysis

\begin{theorem}
The equation:
:$e^x \paren {x - 1} = e^{-x} \paren {x + 1}$
has a root:
:$x = 1 \cdotp 19966 \, 78640 \, 25773 \, 4 \ldots$
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $\map f x = e^x \paren {x - 1} - e^{-x} \paren {x + 1}$.
Then if $\map f c = 0$, $c$ is a root of $e^x \paren {x - 1} = e^{-x} \paren {x + 1}$.
Notice that:
:$\map f 1 = e^1 \paren {1 - 1} - e^{-1} \paren {1 + 1} = -\dfrac 2 e < 0$
:$\map f 2 = e^2 \paren {2 - 1} - e^{-2} \paren {2 + 1} = e^2 - \dfrac 3 {e^2} > 0$
By Intermediate Value Theorem:
:$\exists c \in \openint 1 2: \map f c = 0$.
This shows that our equation has a root between $1$ and $2$.
The exact value of this root can be found using any numerical method, e.g. Newton's Method.
{{ProofWanted|Analysis needed of the Kepler Equation}}
\end{proof}

\section{Intermediate Value Theorem}
Tags: Proofs, Named Theorems, Analysis

\begin{theorem}
Let $f: S \to \R$ be a real function on some subset $S$ of $\R$.
Let $I \subseteq S$ be a real interval.
Let $f: I \to \R$ be continuous on $I$.
Then $f$ is a Darboux function.
That is:
Let $a, b \in I$.
Let $k \in \R$ lie between $\map f a$ and $\map f b$.
That is, either:
:$\map f a < k < \map f b$
or:
:$\map f b < k < \map f a$
Then $\exists c \in \openint a b$ such that $\map f c = k$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
This theorem is a restatement of Image of Interval by Continuous Function is Interval.
From Image of Interval by Continuous Function is Interval, the image of $\openint a b$ under $f$ is also a real interval (but not necessarily open).
Thus if $k$ lies between $\map f a$ and $\map f b$, it must be the case that:
:$k \in \Img {\openint a b}$
The result follows.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}

\section{Intermediate Value Theorem/Corollary}
Tags: Named Theorems, Analysis

\begin{theorem}
Let $I$ be a real interval.
Let $a, b \in I$ such that $\openint a b$ is an open interval.
Let $f: I \to \R$ be a real function which is continuous on $\openint a b$.
Let $0 \in \R$ lie between $\map f a$ and $\map f b$.
That is, either:
:$\map f a < 0 < \map f b$
or:
:$\map f b < 0 < \map f a$
Then $f$ has a root in $\openint a b$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Follows directly from the Intermediate Value Theorem and from the definition of root.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}

\section{Infinite Ramsey's Theorem implies Finite Ramsey's Theorem}
Tags: Ramsey Theory

\begin{theorem}
:$\forall l, n, r \in \N: \exists m \in \N: m \to \left({l}\right)_r^n$
where $\alpha \to \left({\beta}\right)^n_r$ means that:
:for any assignment of $r$-colors to the $n$-subsets of $\alpha$
::there is a particular color $\gamma$ and a subset $X$ of $\alpha$ of size $\beta$ such that all $n$-subsets of $X$ are $\gamma$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
{{AimForCont}} there is a $l$ such that:
:$\forall m \in \N: m \nrightarrow \left({l}\right)_r^n$
Let $\hat{K_i}$ denote a hypergraph on $i$ vertices where all possible $n$-subsets of the vertices are the hyperedges.
Let $G$ be a hypergraph with vertices $V = \left\{ {v_i: i \in \N}\right\}$.
Let the hyperedges of $G <$ be enumerated by:
:$E = \left\{ {E_i: E_i \subset \N, \left\vert{E_i}\right\vert = n}\right\}$
{{explain|What does $G <$ mean?}}
We construct a (rooted) tree $T$ as follows:
$(1): \quad$ First introduce a root node $r t$.
$(2): \quad$ Each node is allowed to have at most $r<$ children which correspond to the $r$-colors, subject to it satisfying the criteria:
{{explain|What does $r <$ mean?}}
:::A child is always labeled by one among the $r$-colors.
:::Let the colors be denoted $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_r$.
$(3): \quad$ A child $c_i$ is '''permitted''' {{iff}} its introduction creates a path of some finite length $k$ starting from the root.
:::So, if the hyperedges $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k$ are colored by the colors used in the path in the same order, then the corresponding subgraph of $G$ does not contain a monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$.
:::For example: if the introduction of a child $c_i$ creates the $k$ length path $r t, c_a, c_b, \ldots, c_i$ and the hyperedges $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k$ when colored $c_a, c_b, \ldots, c_i$ do not contain a monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$, the child $c_i$ is permitted to be added to $T$.
Note that for all $m$, there always exists a coloring of $\hat{K_m}$ such that no monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$ exists within.
Thus the situation that a child cannot be added to any vertex at a given level $k$ cannot arise.
For we can always take a coloring of $\hat{K_{k+n}}$ containing no monochromatic $\hat{K_l}$.
Since any $k$ hyperedges in it would yield a sequence of colors already existing in $T$, we know which vertex to add the child to.
We give the child the color corresponding to any other edge.
Hence we can forever keep adding children and so $T$ is infinite.
It is also obvious that each level $k$ of $T$ has at most $r^k<$ vertices.
So each level is finite.
By König's Tree Lemma there will be an infinite path $P$ in $T$.
$P$ provides a $r$-coloring of $G$ that contains no monochromatic $\hat{K_i}$.
Hence $P$ contains no monochromatic infinite hypergraph.
This contradicts the Infinite Ramsey's Theorem.
The result follows by Proof by Contradiction.
{{qed}}
Category:Ramsey Theory
\end{proof}

The problem asks whether there exists a real number \(x\) such that \(\cos(x) = x^{2023}\), where \(\cos(x)\) represents magic power and \(x^{2023}\) represents physical power.

To solve this, define the function \(f(x) = \cos(x) - x^{2023}\). A solution exists if there is some \(x\) where \(f(x) = 0\).

### Step 1: Continuity of \(f(x)\)
- The function \(\cos(x)\) is continuous for all real \(x\).
- The function \(x^{2023}\) is continuous for all real \(x\) because 2023 is an odd integer, and power functions with integer exponents are continuous everywhere.
- Since \(f(x)\) is the difference of two continuous functions, \(f(x)\) is continuous for all real \(x\).

### Step 2: Application of the Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
The Intermediate Value Theorem (Document 4) states that if a function \(f\) is continuous on a closed interval \([a, b]\), and \(k\) is any value between \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\), then there exists some \(c \in (a, b)\) such that \(f(c) = k\). Specifically, if \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there is a root in \((a, b)\) (Document 5).

Evaluate \(f(x)\) at two points:
- At \(x = 0\):  
  \(f(0) = \cos(0) - 0^{2023} = 1 - 0 = 1 > 0\).
- At \(x = 1\):  
  \(\cos(1) \approx 0.5403\) and \(1^{2023} = 1\), so \(f(1) \approx 0.5403 - 1 = -0.4597 < 0\).

Thus:
- \(f(0) = 1 > 0\)
- \(f(1) \approx -0.4597 < 0\)

Since \(f(x)\) is continuous on \([0, 1]\), and \(f(0)\) and \(f(1)\) have opposite signs, the IVT guarantees there exists some \(c \in (0, 1)\) such that \(f(c) = 0\).

### Conclusion
There exists a real number \(x \in (0, 1)\) where \(\cos(x) = x^{2023}\), meaning the character's magic and physical powers are equal.

\boxed{\text{yes}}

question:
In a drawing competition, two teams are asked to draw two different shapes, Team A draws a five-sided figure (pentagon) and Team B draws another five-sided figure. Both figures have the same shape but different sizes. Team A's figure has sides of lengths 8, 8, 4, 4, and 6 units, with two sides being equal to each other in pairs. Team B's figure has two sides of length 3 units each and another two sides of equal length, but the exact measurement isn't given. If the figures are considered to have the same proportions, what is the total length of the sides of Team A's figure?

\begin{definition}[Definition:Solid of Revolution]
Let $F$ be a plane figure.
Let $L$ be a straight line in the same plane as $F$.
Let $F$ be rotated one full turn about $L$ in $3$-dimensional space.
The '''solid of revolution generated by $F$ around $L$''' is the solid figure whose boundaries are the paths taken by the sides of $F$ as they go round $L$.
The usual scenario is that:
: $(1): \quad$ one of the sides of $F$ is a straight line coincident with $L$
: $(2): \quad$ $L$ itself is aligned with one of the coordinate axes, usually the $x$-axis in a cartesian plane
: $(3): \quad$ Two other sides of $F$ are also straight lines, perpendicular to $L$
: $(4): \quad$ The remaining side (or sides) of $F$ are curved and described by means of a real function.
:500px
The above diagram shows a typical '''solid of revolution'''.
The plane figure $ABCD$ has been rotated $2 \pi$ radians around the $x$-axis.
$FECD$ illustrates the disposition of $ABCD$ part way round.
\end{definition}

[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Reciprocal Proportion]
Let $P$ and $Q$ be geometric figures of the same type (that is, having the same number and configuration of sides).
Let $A$ and $B$ be sides of $P$, and let $C$ and $D$ be sides of $Q$, such that $A$ and $C$ are corresponding sides, and $B$ and $D$ also be corresponding sides.
Then $P$ and $Q$ have sides which are '''in reciprocal proportion''', or are '''reciprocally proportional''', if:
:$A : D = B : C$
where $A : D$ is the ratio of the lengths of $A$ and $D$.
\end{definition}

\section{Smallest Square Inscribed in Two Pythagorean Triangles}
Tags: Pythagorean Triangles

\begin{theorem}
The smallest square with integer sides that can be inscribed within two different Pythagorean triangles so that one side of the square lies on the hypotenuse has side length $780$.
The two Pythagorean triangles in question have side lengths $\tuple {1443, 1924, 2405}$ and $\tuple {1145, 2748, 2977}$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
By Inscribed Squares in Right-Angled Triangle/Side Lengths/Side Lies on Hypotenuse:
:For a Pythagorean triangle with side lengths $a, b, c$, the required inscribed square has side length given by:
::$\dfrac {abc}{ab + c^2}$
For primitive Pythagorean triples, $a, b, c$ are pairwise coprime, so the above fraction is in canonical form.
In other words, if the required side length is an integer, the triangle cannot be primitive, and this side length would be equal to some multiple of the product $abc$ of its primitive version.
Therefore in order to find the smallest such square, we would need to compare and find two sets of primitive Pythagorean triples such that their product would have a sufficiently small lowest common multiple.
The $\tuple {3, 4, 5}$ triple has a product of $60$.
The $\tuple {5, 12, 13}$ triple has a product of $780$.
These two products have a lowest common multiple of $780$.
Since $\sqrt [3] {780} < 10$, there is no need to search any further for triangles with smallest side length greater than $10$.
The ones remaining are:
:$7, 24, 25$
:$8, 15, 17$
:$9, 40, 41$
which all have products greater than $780$.
Therefore the solution must be generated with the two smallest triangles.
The inscribed square side length for the $\tuple {3, 4, 5}$ triangle is:
:$\dfrac {60} {3 \times 4 + 5^2} = \dfrac {60} {37}$
so it must be enlarged $481$ times to have a side length of $780$.
The inscribed square side length for the $\tuple {5, 12, 13}$ triangle is:
:$\dfrac {780} {5 \times 12 + 13^2} = \dfrac {780} {229}$
so it must be enlarged $229$ times to have a side length of $780$.
After enlargement, we get the triples $\tuple {1443, 1924, 2405}$ and $\tuple {1145, 2748, 2977}$, which is the result we have.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 4]
\section{Triangle with Two Equal Angles is Isosceles}
Tags: Triangles, Isosceles Triangles, Triangle with Two Equal Angles is Isosceles, Euclid Book I

\begin{theorem}
If a triangle has two angles equal to each other, the sides which subtend the equal angles will also be equal to one another.
Hence, by definition, such a triangle will be isosceles.
{{:Euclid:Proposition/I/6}}
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
:200px
Let $\triangle ABC$ be a triangle in which $\angle ABC = \angle ACB$.
Suppose side $AB$ is not equal to side $AC$. Then one of them will be greater.
Suppose $AB > AC$.
We cut off from $AB$ a length $DB$ equal to $AC$.
We draw the line segment $CD$.
Since $DB = AC$, and $BC$ is common, the two sides $DB, BC$ are equal to $AC, CB$ respectively.
Also, $\angle DBC = \angle ACB$.
So by Triangle Side-Angle-Side Equality, $\triangle DBC = \triangle ACB$.
But $\triangle DBC$ is smaller than $\triangle ACB$, which is absurd.
Therefore, have $AB \le AC$. 
A similar argument shows the converse, and hence $AB = AC$.
{{qed}}
{{Euclid Note|6|I|It is the converse of Proposition $5$: Isosceles Triangle has Two Equal Angles.}}
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Congruence (Geometry)]
In the field of Euclidean geometry, two geometric figures are '''congruent''' if they are, informally speaking, both "the same size and shape".
That is, one figure can be overlaid on the other figure with a series of rotations, translations, and reflections.
Specifically:
:all corresponding angles of the '''congruent''' figures must have the same measurement
:all corresponding sides of the '''congruent''' figures must be be the same length.
\end{definition}

question:
Imagine you're a scientist studying a special function f that is bounded and entire. You're analyzing how this signal interacts with two specific points on the radius-r ball. What is the value of the integral $\int_{|z|=r} f(z)/(z-z_1)(z-z_2) dz$?

[Document 1]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Valid Time Indication]
Consider an analogue clock with an hour hand $H$ and a minute hand $M$.
A '''valid time indication''' is an arrangement of $H$ and $M$ which indicates a specific time of day (either a.m. or p.m.).
{{handwaving|It is not at all clear what it means to "indicate a specific time of day" since we have not defined how a clock works. I can imagine we don't want to go that far because it would be beside the point, but then we should explain that it is different from a random orientation of H and M which does not correspond to normal operation of a clock.}}
Category:Definitions/Clocks
\end{definition}

[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Set/Uniqueness of Elements/Multiple Specification]
For a given '''set''', an object is either '''in''' the set or '''not in''' the set.
So, if an element is in a set, then it is in the set '''only once''', however many times it may appear in the definition of the set.
Thus, the set $\set {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4}$ is the same set as $\set {1, 2, 3, 4}$.
$2$ and $3$ are in the set, and '''listing''' them twice makes no difference to the set's '''contents'''.
Like the membership of a club, if you're in, you're in -- however many membership cards you have to prove it.
\end{definition}

[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Infinity]
Informally, the term '''infinity''' is used to mean '''some infinite number''', but this concept falls very far short of a usable definition.
The symbol $\infty$ (supposedly invented by {{AuthorRef|John Wallis}}) is often used in this context to mean '''an infinite number'''.
However, outside of its formal use in the definition of limits its use is strongly discouraged until you know what you're talking about.
It is defined as having the following properties:
:$\forall n \in \Z: n < \infty$
:$\forall n \in \Z: n + \infty = \infty$
:$\forall n \in \Z: n \times \infty = \infty$
:$\infty^2 = \infty$
Similarly, the quantity written as $-\infty$ is defined as having the following properties:
:$\forall n \in \Z: -\infty< n$
:$\forall n \in \Z: -\infty + n = -\infty$
:$\forall n \in \Z: -\infty \times n = -\infty$
:$\paren {-\infty}^2 = -\infty$
The latter result seems wrong when you think of the rule that a negative number square equals a positive one, but remember that infinity is not exactly a number as such.
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Primitive (Calculus)/Indefinite Integral]
Suppose that the real or complex function $F$ is a primitive of the function $f$.
From the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, it is apparent that to find the value of a definite integral for a function between two points, one can find the value of the primitive of the function at those points and subtract one from the other.
Thus arises the notation:
:$\ds \int \map f x \rd x = \map F x + C$
where $C$ is the arbitrary constant.
In this context, the expression $\ds \int \map f x \rd x$ is known as the '''indefinite integral''' of $f$.
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\section{Cauchy-Goursat Theorem}
Tags: Complex Analysis

\begin{theorem}
Let $D$ be a simply connected open subset of the complex plane $\C$.
Let $\partial D$ denote the closed contour bounding $D$.
Let $f: D \to \C$ be holomorphic everywhere in $D$.
Then:
:$\ds \oint_{\partial D} \map f z \rd z = 0$
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Begin by rewriting the function $f$ and differential $\rd z$ in terms of their real and complex parts:
:$f = u + iv$
:$\d z = \d x + i \rd y$
Then we have:
:$\ds \oint_{\partial D} \map f z \rd z = \oint_{\partial D} \paren {u + iv} \paren {\d x + i \rd y}$
Expanding the result and again separating into real and complex parts yields two integrals of real variables:
:$\ds \oint_{\partial D} \paren {u \rd x - v \rd y} + i \oint_{\partial D} \paren {v \rd x + u \rd y}$
We next apply Green's Theorem to each integral term to convert the contour integrals to surface integrals over $D$:
:$\ds \iint_D \paren {-\dfrac {\partial v} {\partial x} - \dfrac {\partial u} {\partial y} } \rd x \rd y + \iint_D \paren {\dfrac {\partial u} {\partial x} - \dfrac {\partial v} {\partial y} } \rd x \rd y$
By the assumption that $f$ is holomorphic, it satisfies the Cauchy-Riemann Equations
:$\dfrac {\partial v} {\partial x} + \dfrac {\partial u} {\partial y} = 0$
:$\dfrac {\partial u} {\partial x} - \dfrac {\partial v} {\partial y} = 0$
The integrands are therefore zero and hence the integral is zero.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}

The function \(f(z)\) is given to be bounded and entire. By Liouville's theorem, any bounded entire function must be constant. Therefore, \(f(z) = c\) for some constant \(c\).

The integral to evaluate is \(\int_{|z|=r} \frac{f(z)}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)}  dz\), where \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) are specific points on the radius-\(r\) ball. The phrase "on the radius-\(r\) ball" is interpreted to mean that \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) lie within the closed disk \(|z| \leq r\), as the term "ball" in a two-dimensional context refers to a disk. However, if \(z_1\) or \(z_2\) lie on the contour \(|z| = r\), the integral may be improper. Despite this, the result holds in all cases as shown below.

Substituting \(f(z) = c\), the integral becomes:
\[
c \int_{|z|=r} \frac{dz}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)}.
\]

Consider the cases based on the locations of \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) relative to the contour.

### Case 1: Both \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) are inside the disk (\(|z_1| < r\), \(|z_2| < r\))
The integrand has poles at \(z_1\) and \(z_2\). By the residue theorem, the integral over the closed contour \(|z| = r\) (traversed counterclockwise) is \(2\pi i\) times the sum of the residues inside the contour.

The residue at \(z = z_1\) is:
\[
\lim_{z \to z_1} (z - z_1) \cdot \frac{c}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)} = \frac{c}{z_1 - z_2}.
\]

The residue at \(z = z_2\) is:
\[
\lim_{z \to z_2} (z - z_2) \cdot \frac{c}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)} = \frac{c}{z_2 - z_1} = -\frac{c}{z_1 - z_2}.
\]

Sum of residues:
\[
\frac{c}{z_1 - z_2} - \frac{c}{z_1 - z_2} = 0.
\]

Thus, the integral is \(2\pi i \cdot 0 = 0\).

### Case 2: \(z_1 = z_2\) (inside or on the contour)
If \(z_1 = z_2 = a\), the integrand is \(\frac{c}{(z - a)^2}\), which has a double pole at \(z = a\).

The residue at a double pole is:
\[
\lim_{z \to a} \frac{d}{dz} \left[ (z - a)^2 \cdot \frac{c}{(z - a)^2} \right] = \lim_{z \to a} \frac{d}{dz} c = 0,
\]
since the derivative of a constant is zero.

- If \(a\) is inside (\(|a| < r\)), the residue theorem gives \(2\pi i \cdot 0 = 0\).
- If \(a\) is on the contour (\(|a| = r\)), the principal value integral (discussed below) is also 0.

### Case 3: \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) distinct and on the contour (\(|z_1| = r\), \(|z_2| = r\), \(z_1 \neq z_2\))
The integral is improper due to poles on the contour. The principal value (PV) is considered, defined by indenting the contour with small semicircles around each pole and taking the limit as the radius \(\varepsilon \to 0\).

The sum of the residues of \(\frac{1}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)}\) at \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) is:
\[
\frac{1}{z_1 - z_2} + \frac{1}{z_2 - z_1} = \frac{1}{z_1 - z_2} - \frac{1}{z_1 - z_2} = 0.
\]

For a simple pole on a contour, the principal value integral satisfies:
\[
\text{PV} \int \frac{dz}{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)} = i \pi \times (\text{sum of residues}) = i \pi \cdot 0 = 0.
\]
Thus, the principal value integral is 0, and since \(f(z) = c\) is constant, the original integral (interpreted as principal value) is \(c \cdot 0 = 0\).

In all cases, the integral evaluates to 0. The boundedness and entire nature of \(f\) ensure it is constant, and the specific locations of \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) (inside or on the contour) do not change the result.

\[
\boxed{0}
\]

question:
Imagine launching a toy rocket straight up into the sky, and its path through the air can be tracked with a formula based on time since launch. The formula for its flight path is given by the equation where the height of the rocket at any time 't' (in seconds) is determined by $h=200t - 4.9t^{2}$, while the horizontal distance at any time 't' is determined by 'd=80t'. How high does the rocket go at its highest point before it starts to come back down?

\section{Acceleration of Rocket in Outer Space}
Tags: Rocket Science, Dynamics

\begin{theorem}
Let $B$ be a rocket travelling in outer space.
Let the velocity of $B$ at time $t$ be $\mathbf v$.
Let the mass of $B$ at time $t$ be $m$.
Let the exhaust velocity of $B$ be constant at $\mathbf b$.
Then the acceleration of $B$ at time $t$ is given by:
:$\mathbf a = \dfrac 1 m \paren {-\mathbf b \dfrac {\d m} {\d t} }$
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
From Motion of Rocket in Outer Space, the equation of motion of $B$ is given by:
:$(1): \quad m \dfrac {\d \mathbf v} {\d t} = -\mathbf b \dfrac {\d m} {\d t}$
By definition, the acceleration of $B$ is its rate of change of velocity:
:$\mathbf a = \dfrac {\d \mathbf v} {\d t}$
The result follows by substituting $\mathbf a$ for $\dfrac {\d \mathbf v} {\d t}$ and dividing by $m$.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Burnout Height]
Let $R$ be a rocket which is propelled by backwards expulsion of the exhaust products of the burning of its internal fuel supply.
Let $R$ be fired straight up from the surface of a planet.
The '''burnout height''' of $R$ is the height attained by $R$ at the instant when its fuel has become exhausted.
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Travelling With and Against the Flow Puzzle]
A '''travelling with and against the flow puzzle''' is a problem of the form:
:A body travels a certain distance $d$ through a medium in the same direction as the constant flow of that medium in a given time $t_1$.
:The same body travels the same distance $d$ in the opposite direction, that is against the flow of the medium, in a given time $t_2$.
:How long would it take the body to travel $d$ in absence of any such flow?
Variants give:
:the time taken for the body to travel at the same speed as the medium
:the time taken for the body to travel either with or against the medium
where the object of the exercise is to work out how long it takes to travel in the opposite direction.
Category:Definitions/Classic Problems
\end{definition}

The height of the rocket at any time \(t\) (in seconds) is given by the equation \(h = 200t - 4.9t^2\), where \(h\) is in meters. This equation represents the vertical motion of the rocket under constant gravity, with an initial vertical velocity of 200 m/s and gravitational acceleration such that \(\frac{1}{2}g = 4.9\), so \(g = 9.8\) m/s².

The maximum height occurs when the vertical velocity is zero. The vertical velocity is the derivative of the height function with respect to time:
\[
v(t) = \frac{dh}{dt} = 200 - 9.8t.
\]
Set \(v(t) = 0\) to find the time at maximum height:
\[
200 - 9.8t = 0 \implies t = \frac{200}{9.8} = \frac{2000}{98} = \frac{1000}{49} \text{ seconds}.
\]

Substitute this time back into the height equation to find the maximum height:
\[
h = 200 \left( \frac{1000}{49} \right) - 4.9 \left( \frac{1000}{49} \right)^2.
\]

First, compute \(200 \times \frac{1000}{49} = \frac{200000}{49}\).

Next, compute \(\left( \frac{1000}{49} \right)^2 = \frac{1000000}{2401}\), and since \(2401 = 49 \times 49\),
\[
4.9 \times \frac{1000000}{2401} = \frac{49}{10} \times \frac{1000000}{49 \times 49} = \frac{1000000}{10 \times 49} = \frac{100000}{49}.
\]

Now, substitute these values:
\[
h = \frac{200000}{49} - \frac{100000}{49} = \frac{200000 - 100000}{49} = \frac{100000}{49} \text{ meters}.
\]

The fraction \(\frac{100000}{49}\) is in simplest form, as 100000 and 49 have no common factors (49 is \(7^2\) and 100000 is \(2^5 \times 5^5\)).

The horizontal distance equation \(d = 80t\) is not relevant for finding the maximum height, as the vertical motion determines the height independently.

Thus, the maximum height the rocket reaches is \(\frac{100000}{49}\) meters.

\boxed{\dfrac{100000}{49}}

question:
Imagine you have a digital scale that can measure the weight of an infinite number of infinitely small digital dots. Each dot can either be on or off, and their weights are determined by a specific pattern: the first dot weighs 1/4 gram, the second dot weighs 1/16 grams, the third dot weighs 1/64 grams, and so on, with each subsequent dot weighing exactly one-fourth the weight of the previous dot. If you can create any combination of these dots being on (weighing their specified amount) or off (weighing nothing), and you can also add a whole number of grams to the scale (from a set of weights that are whole grams), what would be the total possible weight measured by the scale? (specifically, Lebesgue measure of this sum)

Retrieved documents:
[Document 1]
\section{Rational Numbers Null Set under Lebesgue Measure}
Tags: Measure Theory, Lebesgue Measure

\begin{theorem}
Let $\lambda$ be $1$-dimensional Lebesgue measure on $\R$.
Let $\Q$ be the set of rational numbers.
Then:
:$\map \lambda \Q = 0$
that is, $\Q$ is a $\lambda$-null set.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We have that the Rational Numbers are Countably Infinite.
The result follows from Countable Set is Null Set under Lebesgue Measure.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 2]
\section{Cantor Set has Zero Lebesgue Measure}
Tags: Cantor Set, Measure Theory, Lebesgue Measure, Cantor Space

\begin{theorem}
Let $\CC$ be the Cantor set.
Let $\lambda$ denote the Lebesgue measure on the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\map \BB \R$ on $\R$.
Then $\CC$ is $\map \BB \R$-measurable, and $\map \lambda \CC = 0$.
That is, $\CC$ is a $\lambda$-null set.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Consider the definition of $\CC$ as a limit of a decreasing sequence.
In the notation as introduced there, we see that each $S_n$ is a collection of disjoint closed intervals.
From Closed Set Measurable in Borel Sigma-Algebra, these are measurable sets.
Furthermore, each $S_n$ is finite.
Hence by Sigma-Algebra Closed under Union, it follows that $C_n := \ds \bigcup S_n$ is measurable as well.
Then, as we have:
:$\CC = \ds \bigcap_{n \mathop \in \N} C_n$
it follows from Sigma-Algebra Closed under Countable Intersection that $\CC$ is measurable.
The $C_n$ also form a decreasing sequence of sets with limit $\CC$.
Thus, from Characterization of Measures: $(3')$, it follows that:
:$\map \lambda \CC = \ds \lim_{n \mathop \to \infty} \map \lambda {C_n}$
It is not too hard to show that, for all $n \in \N$:
:$\map \lambda {C_n} = \paren {\dfrac 2 3}^n$
{{finish|yes, I know}}
Now we have by Sequence of Powers of Number less than One that:
:$\ds \lim_{n \mathop \to \infty} \paren {\frac 2 3}^n = 0$
and the result follows.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Lebesgue Measure]
Let $\JJ_{ho}^n$ be the set of half-open $n$-rectangles.
Let $\map \BB {\R^n}$ be the Borel $\sigma$-algebra on $\R^n$.
Let $\lambda^n$ be the $n$-dimensional Lebesgue pre-measure on $\JJ_{ho}^n$.
Any measure $\mu$ extending $\lambda^n$ to $\map \BB {\R^n}$ is called '''$n$-dimensional Lebesgue measure'''.
That is, $\mu$ is an '''$n$-dimensional Lebesgue measure''' {{iff}} it satisfies:
:$\mu \restriction_{\JJ_{ho}^n} = \lambda^n$
where $\restriction$ denotes restriction.
Usually, this measure is also denoted by $\lambda^n$, even though this may be considered abuse of notation.
{{refactor|Below characterisation of Lebesgue measure $\lambda^1$ is reminiscent of the proof of Carathéodory's Theorem (Measure Theory) as discussed on User:Lord_Farin/Sandbox/Archive, and I wonder if it adds anything}}
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Weighted Mean/Normalized]
Let $S = \sequence {x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n}$ be a sequence of real numbers.
Let $\map W x$ be a weight function to be applied to the terms of $S$.
Let the weights be normalized.
Then the '''weighted mean''' of $S$ can be expressed in the form:
:$\ds \bar x := \sum_{i \mathop = 1}^n \map W {x_i} x_i$
as by definition of normalized weight function all the weights add up to $1$.
Category:Definitions/Measures of Central Tendency
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\section{Lebesgue Measure is Diffuse}
Tags: Measure Theory, Lebesgue Measure, Diffuse Measures

\begin{theorem}
Let $\lambda^n$ be Lebesgue measure on $\R^n$.
Then $\lambda^n$ is a diffuse measure.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
A singleton $\set {\mathbf x} \subseteq \R^n$ is seen to be closed by combining:
:Euclidean Space is Complete Metric Space
:Metric Space is Hausdorff
:Corollary to Compact Subspace of Hausdorff Space is Closed
Hence by Closed Set Measurable in Borel Sigma-Algebra:
:$\set {\mathbf x} \in \map \BB {\R^n}$
where $\map \BB {\R^n}$ is the Borel $\sigma$-algebra on $\R^n$.
Write $\mathbf x + \epsilon = \tuple {x_1 + \epsilon, \ldots, x_n + \epsilon}$ for $\epsilon > 0$.
Then:
:$\ds \set {\mathbf x} = \bigcap_{m \mathop \in \N} \horectr {\mathbf x} {\mathbf x + \frac 1 m}$
where $\\horectr {\mathbf x} {\mathbf x + \dfrac 1 m}$ is a half-open $n$-rectangle.
{{handwaving|justify equality}}
By definition of Lebesgue measure, we have (for all $m \in \N$):
:$\ds \map {\lambda^n} {\horectr {\mathbf x} {\mathbf x + \frac 1 m} } = \prod_{i \mathop = 1}^n \frac 1 m = m^{-n}$
From Characterization of Measures, it follows that:
:$\ds \map {\lambda^n} {\set {\mathbf x} } = \lim_{m \mathop \to \infty} m^{-n}$
which equals $0$ from Sequence of Powers of Reciprocals is Null Sequence.
Therefore, for each $\mathbf x \in \R^n$:
:$\map {\lambda^n} {\set {\mathbf x} } = 0$
that is, $\lambda^n$ is a diffuse measure.
{{qed}}
\end{proof}

The set of possible weights measured by the scale consists of all numbers of the form \(k + s\), where \(k\) is a non-negative integer (a whole number of grams added) and \(s\) is the sum of a subset of the digital dots. The weight of the \(n\)th dot is \(\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n\) grams, so \(s\) belongs to the set \(S\) defined as:
\[
S = \left\{ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \epsilon_n \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n \mid \epsilon_n \in \{0,1\} \right\}.
\]
This set \(S\) is the collection of all numbers in \([0, \frac{1}{3}]\) that have a base-4 expansion using only the digits 0 and 1. The set \(S\) is a Cantor-like set, similar to the standard Cantor set but constructed with base-4 expansions restricted to digits 0 and 1.

The Lebesgue measure of \(S\) is zero. This can be shown through self-similarity: \(S\) is the disjoint union of two sets:
- \(S_0 = \left\{ \frac{y}{4} \mid y \in S \right\}\), which is a scaled version of \(S\) by a factor of \(\frac{1}{4}\).
- \(S_1 = \left\{ \frac{1}{4} + \frac{z}{4} \mid z \in S \right\}\), which is a translate and scaled version of \(S\) by a factor of \(\frac{1}{4}\).

The Lebesgue measure is translation-invariant and scales with linear transformations, so:
\[
\lambda(S_0) = \lambda\left(\frac{S}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4} \lambda(S), \quad \lambda(S_1) = \lambda\left(\frac{1 + S}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4} \lambda(S).
\]
Since \(S = S_0 \cup S_1\) and the sets are disjoint:
\[
\lambda(S) = \lambda(S_0) + \lambda(S_1) = \frac{1}{4} \lambda(S) + \frac{1}{4} \lambda(S) = \frac{1}{2} \lambda(S).
\]
This equation implies that \(\lambda(S) = 0\).

The total set of possible weights is:
\[
T = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} (k + S).
\]
Each set \(k + S\) is a translate of \(S\), and since Lebesgue measure is translation-invariant, \(\lambda(k + S) = \lambda(S) = 0\). The sets \(k + S\) are disjoint because \(k + S \subseteq [k, k + \frac{1}{3}]\) and \([k, k + \frac{1}{3}] \cap [k+1, k+1 + \frac{1}{3}] = \emptyset\) for all integers \(k \geq 0\), as \(k + \frac{1}{3} < k+1\).

The Lebesgue measure of a countable union of disjoint sets is the sum of their measures:
\[
\lambda(T) = \lambda\left( \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} (k + S) \right) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \lambda(k + S) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 0 = 0.
\]
Thus, the Lebesgue measure of the set of all possible weights is 0.

question:
Imagine you're painting a mural on a long, straight wall. The wall is divided into 4 equal sections. The height of the mural at the beginning of the wall is 3 feet, then 7 feet at the next quarter, 11 feet at the halfway point, 9 feet three-quarters down the wall, and finally, it goes back down to 3 feet at the end of the wall. If you were to calculate the total painted area of the mural using the average height of each section, what would the approximate area be?

Retrieved documents:
[Document 1]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Feuerbach Circle]
Let $\triangle ABC$ be a triangle.
The '''Feuerbach circle''' of $\triangle ABC$ is the circle which passes through each of the $9$ points:
: the feet of the altitudes of $\triangle ABC$
: the midpoints of the sides of $\triangle ABC$
: the midpoints of the lines from the vertices of $\triangle ABC$ to the orthocenter of $\triangle ABC$.
:500px
\end{definition}

[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Continuous Real Function/Closed Interval]
Let $f$ be a real function defined on a closed interval $\closedint a b$.
$f$ is '''continuous on $\closedint a b$''' {{iff}} it is:
:$(1): \quad$ continuous at every point of the open interval $\openint a b$
:$(2): \quad$ continuous on the right at $a$
:$(3): \quad$ continuous on the left at $b$.
That is, if $f$ is to be continuous over the ''whole'' of a closed interval, it needs to be continuous at the end points.
Because we only have "access" to the function on one side of each end point, all we can do is insist on continuity on the side of the end points on which the function is defined.
\end{definition}

[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Abel's Integral Equation]
'''Abel's integral equation''' is an integral equation whose purpose is to solve Abel's mechanical problem, which finds how long it will take a bead to slide down a wire.
The purpose of '''Abel's integral equation''' is to find the shape of the curve into which the wire is bent in order to yield that result:
Let $\map T y$ be a function which specifies the total time of descent for a given starting height.
:$\ds \map T {y_0} = \int_{y \mathop = y_0}^{y \mathop = 0} \rd t = \frac 1 {\sqrt {2 g} } \int_0^{y_0} \frac 1 {\sqrt {y_0 - y} } \frac {\d s} {\d y} \rd y$
where:
:$y$ is the height of the bead at time $t$
:$y_0$ is the height from which the bead is released
:$g$ is Acceleration Due to Gravity
:$\map s y$ is the distance along the curve as a function of height.
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\section{Area of Trapezoid}
Tags: Trapezoids, Areas of Quadrilaterals, Area Formulas, Quadrilaterals

\begin{theorem}
:410px
Let $ABCD$ be a trapezoid:
:whose parallel sides are of lengths $a$ and $b$
and
:whose height is $h$.
Then the area of $ABCD$ is given by:
:$\Box ABCD = \dfrac {h \paren {a + b} } 2$
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
:600px
Extend line $AB$ to $E$ by length $a$.
Extend line $DC$ to $F$ by length $b$.
Then $BECF$ is another trapezoid whose parallel sides are of lengths $a$ and $b$ and whose height is $h$.
Also, $AEFD$ is a parallelogram which comprises the two trapezoids $ABCD$ and $BECF$.
So $\Box ABCD + \Box BECF = \Box AEFD$ and $\Box ABCD = \Box BECF$.
$AEFD$ is of altitude $h$ with sides of length $a + b$.
Thus from Area of Parallelogram the area of $AEFD$ is given by:
: $\Box AEFD = h \paren {a + b}$
It follows that $\Box ABCD = \dfrac {h \paren {a + b} } 2$
{{qed}}
\end{proof}



[Document 5]
\section{Volume of Cylinder}
Tags: Cylinders, Volume Formulas

\begin{theorem}
The volume $V_C$ of a cylinder whose bases are circles of radius $r$ and whose height is $h$ is given by the formula:
:$V_C = \pi r^2 h$
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
:600px
Consider a cylinder $C$ whose base is a circle of radius $r$ and whose height is $h$.
Consider a cuboid $K$ whose height is $h$ and whose base has the same area as the base of $C$.
Let the area of those bases be $A$.
Let the cylinder $C$ be positioned with its base in the same plane as the base of $K$.
By Cavalieri's Principle $C$ and $K$ have the same volume.
The bases of $C$ are circles of radius $r$.
From Area of Circle, the area of each base therefore gives:
:$A = \pi r^2$
From Volume of Cuboid, $K$ has volume given by:
:$V_K = A h = \pi r^2 h$
Hence the result.
{{qed}}
Category:Cylinders
Category:Volume Formulas
\end{proof}

To calculate the total painted area of the mural using the average height of each section, the wall is divided into four equal sections. The heights at the specified points are:
- Beginning (0%): 3 feet
- First quarter (25%): 7 feet
- Halfway (50%): 11 feet
- Three-quarters (75%): 9 feet
- End (100%): 3 feet

Assuming the height changes linearly between these points, the average height for each section is calculated as the average of the heights at the start and end of the section.

- **Section 1 (0% to 25%)**: Average height = \((3 + 7) / 2 = 5\) feet
- **Section 2 (25% to 50%)**: Average height = \((7 + 11) / 2 = 9\) feet
- **Section 3 (50% to 75%)**: Average height = \((11 + 9) / 2 = 10\) feet
- **Section 4 (75% to 100%)**: Average height = \((9 + 3) / 2 = 6\) feet

The sum of the average heights is \(5 + 9 + 10 + 6 = 30\) feet. Since the wall is divided into four equal sections and the total length is not specified, the length per section is assumed to be 1 foot for the purpose of calculating a numerical approximate area. This assumption is reasonable as the problem requires an approximate numerical area, and the section length is consistent across the wall.

Thus, the area for each section is:
- Section 1: \(5 \times 1 = 5\) square feet
- Section 2: \(9 \times 1 = 9\) square feet
- Section 3: \(10 \times 1 = 10\) square feet
- Section 4: \(6 \times 1 = 6\) square feet

The total approximate area is \(5 + 9 + 10 + 6 = 30\) square feet.

\boxed{30}

question:
A scientist is studying a certain type of bacteria that can survive in extreme conditions. She wants to model the growth of this bacteria population based on two factors: the average size of the population (which she has a specific target for) and the average natural logarithm of the population size (which she also has a target value for). Given these constraints, she's trying to determine the maximum entropy density distribution to use. Which of the following distributions is it? (a) Exponential. (b) Gamma. (c) Beta. (d) Uniform.

\begin{definition}[Definition:Differential Entropy]
'''Differential entropy''' extends the concept of entropy to continuous random variables.
Let $X$ be a continuous random variable.
Let $X$ have probability density function $f_X$. 
Then the '''differential entropy''' of $X$, $\map h X$ measured in nats, is given by: 
:$\ds \map h X = -\int_{-\infty}^\infty \map {f_X} x \ln \map {f_X} x \rd x$
Where $\map {f_X} x = 0$, we take $\map {f_X} x \ln \map {f_X} x = 0$ by convention.
\end{definition}

\section{Upward Löwenheim-Skolem Theorem}
Tags: Mathematical Logic, Model Theory

\begin{theorem}
{{Disambiguate|Definition:Model|I suspect model of a first-order theory $\LL$, which is more specific than what is linked to now}}
Let $T$ be an $\LL$-theory with an infinite model.
Then for each infinite cardinal $\kappa \ge \card \LL$, there exists a model of $T$ with cardinality $\kappa$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The idea is:
:to extend the language by adding $\kappa$ many new constants
and:
:to extend the theory by adding sentences asserting that these constants  are distinct.
It is shown that this new theory is finitely satisfiable using an infinite model of $T$.
Compactness then implies that the new theory has a model.
Some care needs to be taken to ensure that we  construct a model of exactly size $\kappa$.
Let $\LL^*$ be the language formed by adding new constants $\set {c_\alpha: \alpha < \kappa}$ to $\LL$.
Let $T^*$ be the $\LL^*$-theory formed by adding the sentences $\set {c_\alpha \ne c_\beta: \alpha, \beta < \kappa, \ \alpha \ne \beta}$ to $T$.
We show that $T^*$ is finitely satisfiable:
Let $\Delta$ be a finite subset of $T^*$.
Then $\Delta$ contains:
:finitely  many sentences from $T$
along with:
:finitely many sentences of the form $c_\alpha \ne c_\beta$ for the new constant symbols.
Since $T$ has an infinite model, it must have a model $\MM$ of cardinality at most $\card \LL + \aleph_0$.
This model already satisfies everything in $T$.
So, since we can find arbitrarily many distinct elements in it,  it can also be used as a model of $\Delta$ by interpreting the finitely many new constant symbols in $\Delta$ as distinct elements of $\MM$.
Since $T^*$ is finitely satisfiable, it follows by the Compactness Theorem that $T^*$ itself is satisfiable.
Since $T^*$ ensures the existence of $\kappa$ many distinct elements, this means it has models of size at least $\kappa$.
It can be proved separately or observed from the ultraproduct proof of the compactness theorem that $T^*$ then has a model $\MM^*$ of exactly size $\kappa$.
{{explain|That proof needs to be proved, and / or a link needs to be provided to that ultraproduct proof and its implications explained.}}
Since $T^*$ contains $T$, $\MM^*$ is a model of $T$ of size $\kappa$.
{{qed}}
{{Namedfor|Leopold Löwenheim|name2 = Thoralf Albert Skolem|cat = Löwenheim|cat2 = Skolem}}
\end{proof}

We are given a problem about finding the maximum entropy distribution for a bacteria population under two constraints:  
1. The average size of the population (i.e., the expected value of the population size) is fixed.  
2. The average natural logarithm of the population size (i.e., the expected value of the natural log of the population size) is fixed.  

The options are: (a) Exponential, (b) Gamma, (c) Beta, (d) Uniform.  

Recall that the principle of maximum entropy states that, subject to known constraints, the probability distribution that best represents the current state of knowledge is the one with the largest entropy.  

The differential entropy for a continuous random variable is defined as:  
\[ h(X) = -\int f(x) \ln f(x) \, dx \]  
where \( f(x) \) is the probability density function.  

We need to maximize this entropy subject to the constraints:  
1. \( E[X] = \mu \) (some constant)  
2. \( E[\ln X] = \nu \) (some constant)  

Additionally, we have the normalization constraint:  
\[ \int f(x) \, dx = 1 \]  

To solve this, we use the method of Lagrange multipliers. We set up the Lagrangian:  
\[ \mathcal{L} = -\int f(x) \ln f(x) \, dx + \lambda_1 \left( \int f(x) \, dx - 1 \right) + \lambda_2 \left( \int x f(x) \, dx - \mu \right) + \lambda_3 \left( \int \ln x \, f(x) \, dx - \nu \right) \]  

Taking the functional derivative with respect to \( f(x) \) and setting it to zero, we get:  
\[ \frac{\delta \mathcal{L}}{\delta f} = -\ln f(x) - 1 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 x + \lambda_3 \ln x = 0 \]  

So:  
\[ -\ln f(x) - 1 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 x + \lambda_3 \ln x = 0 \]  
\[ \ln f(x) = \lambda_1 - 1 + \lambda_2 x + \lambda_3 \ln x \]  
\[ f(x) = \exp(\lambda_1 - 1) \cdot \exp(\lambda_2 x) \cdot \exp(\lambda_3 \ln x) \]  
\[ f(x) = \exp(\lambda_1 - 1) \cdot x^{\lambda_3} \cdot \exp(\lambda_2 x) \]  

We can rewrite this as:  
\[ f(x) = C \cdot x^{a} \cdot e^{b x} \]  
where \( C = \exp(\lambda_1 - 1) \), \( a = \lambda_3 \), and \( b = \lambda_2 \).  

Now, we recognize that this form is similar to the Gamma distribution. The Gamma distribution has the density:  
\[ f(x; k, \theta) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(k) \theta^k} x^{k-1} e^{-x/\theta} \quad \text{for } x > 0 \]  

We can rewrite our expression as:  
\[ f(x) = C \cdot x^{a} e^{b x} \]  

Note that for the Gamma distribution, we have \( e^{-x/\theta} \), so \( b \) must be negative. Let \( b = -1/\theta \) (so that the exponential decays). Then:  
\[ f(x) = C \cdot x^{a} e^{-x/\theta} \]  

Comparing to the Gamma density, we see that \( a = k-1 \), so \( k = a+1 \). The constant \( C \) must be chosen so that the density integrates to 1. In the Gamma distribution, \( C = \frac{1}{\Gamma(k) \theta^k} \).  

Therefore, the form we have is exactly the Gamma distribution.  

Now, let's check the other options:  

- Exponential: This is a special case of the Gamma distribution (when k=1). But in the exponential distribution, we only have one parameter and typically only constrain the mean. Here we have two constraints (E[X] and E[ln X]), so we need two parameters, which the Gamma provides.  

- Beta: The Beta distribution is defined on [0,1] and has two parameters. Its density is proportional to \( x^{\alpha-1} (1-x)^{\beta-1} \). It does not have an exponential term, so it doesn't match our form.  

- Uniform: The uniform distribution has constant density and maximum entropy when there are no constraints (or only bounds). It does not satisfy the two given constraints in general.  

Therefore, the Gamma distribution is the one that maximizes entropy under the constraints of fixed E[X] and E[ln X].  

But let me confirm: is there any other distribution that might fit? The log-normal distribution has E[ln X] fixed (as the mean of the normal) and E[X] fixed (as a function of the normal parameters), but the maximum entropy distribution under these constraints is not log-normal. In fact, for the log-normal, the entropy is not maximized under these constraints.  

Actually, the Gamma distribution is known to be the maximum entropy distribution for a positive random variable with fixed mean and fixed geometric mean (since E[ln X] relates to the geometric mean).  

Recall: the geometric mean of X is exp(E[ln X]). So we have fixed arithmetic mean and fixed geometric mean.  

And indeed, the Gamma distribution is the maximum entropy distribution under these constraints.  

So the answer should be (b) Gamma.  

But let me double-check the exponential: if we only had the constraint on E[X], then exponential would be maximum entropy. But here we have an additional constraint on E[ln X], so we need a two-parameter family.  

The Gamma distribution has two parameters (k and θ) and can adjust both the mean and the logarithmic expectation.  

For example, the mean is kθ, and E[ln X] = ψ(k) + ln θ, where ψ is the digamma function. So we can solve for k and θ given μ and ν.  

Therefore, the Gamma distribution is the one.  

So the answer is (b) Gamma.

The problem involves finding the maximum entropy distribution for a bacteria population size under two constraints: a fixed average population size (\(E[X] = \mu\)) and a fixed average natural logarithm of the population size (\(E[\ln X] = \nu\)). The maximum entropy distribution maximizes the differential entropy \(h(X) = -\int f(x) \ln f(x)  dx\) subject to these constraints, along with the normalization constraint \(\int f(x)  dx = 1\).

Using the method of Lagrange multipliers, the Lagrangian is:
\[
\mathcal{L} = -\int f(x) \ln f(x)  dx + \lambda_1 \left( \int f(x)  dx - 1 \right) + \lambda_2 \left( \int x f(x)  dx - \mu \right) + \lambda_3 \left( \int \ln x  f(x)  dx - \nu \right).
\]
Taking the functional derivative with respect to \(f(x)\) and setting it to zero gives:
\[
-\ln f(x) - 1 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 x + \lambda_3 \ln x = 0,
\]
which simplifies to:
\[
\ln f(x) = \lambda_1 - 1 + \lambda_2 x + \lambda_3 \ln x.
\]
Exponentiating both sides yields:
\[
f(x) = \exp(\lambda_1 - 1) \cdot x^{\lambda_3} \cdot \exp(\lambda_2 x) = C \cdot x^{a} \cdot e^{b x},
\]
where \(C = \exp(\lambda_1 - 1)\), \(a = \lambda_3\), and \(b = \lambda_2\). For the distribution to be valid, \(b\) must be negative (to ensure integrability), so let \(b = -1/\theta\) with \(\theta > 0\). Then:
\[
f(x) = C \cdot x^{a} \cdot e^{-x/\theta}.
\]
This form matches the **Gamma distribution** density:
\[
f(x; k, \theta) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(k) \theta^k} x^{k-1} e^{-x/\theta}, \quad x > 0,
\]
where \(a = k - 1\) and \(C = \frac{1}{\Gamma(k) \theta^k}\). The Gamma distribution has two parameters (\(k\) and \(\theta\)) that can be adjusted to satisfy the constraints \(E[X] = k\theta = \mu\) and \(E[\ln X] = \psi(k) + \ln \theta = \nu\), where \(\psi\) is the digamma function.

### Evaluation of Other Options:
- **(a) Exponential**: This is a special case of the Gamma distribution when \(k = 1\). However, it has only one parameter and cannot simultaneously satisfy both constraints \(E[X] = \mu\) and \(E[\ln X] = \nu\) in general.
- **(c) Beta**: Defined on \([0, 1]\) with density proportional to \(x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}\). It lacks the exponential term \(e^{b x}\) and is bounded, making it unsuitable for unbounded population sizes and the given constraints.
- **(d) Uniform**: Has constant density and maximizes entropy only when no constraints (other than bounds) are present. It cannot satisfy the specific constraints \(E[X] = \mu\) and \(E[\ln X] = \nu\).

Thus, the Gamma distribution is the maximum entropy distribution under the given constraints.

**Answer: (b) Gamma**

\section{Empty Group Word is Reduced}
Tags: Group Words, Abstract Algebra

\begin{theorem}
Let $S$ be a set
Let $\epsilon$ be the empty group word on $S$.
Then $\epsilon$ is reduced.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
{{improve|Rather than mentioning them in passing and expecting the reader to understand what is meant, we need $w_i$ etc. to be defined. I know from experience that you can't rely on people involved in this website to have adequate knowledge, understanding or deductive abilities to be able to work it out for themselves.}}
By definition, a group word $w = w_1 \cdots w_i \cdots w_n$ is reduced {{iff}} $w_i \ne w_{i + 1}^{-1}$ for all $i \in \set {1, \ldots, n - 1}$, which is vacuously true for $\epsilon$.
{{qed}}
Category:Group Words
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Ramsey Number]
Ramsey's Theorem states that in any coloring of the edges of a sufficiently large complete graph, one will find monochromatic complete subgraphs.
More precisely, for any given number of colors $c$, and any given integers $n_1, \ldots, n_c$, there is a number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ such that:

This number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ is called the '''Ramsey number''' for $n_1, \ldots, n_c$.

\section{Ramsey's Theorem}
Tags: Ramsey Theory, Named Theorems, Combinatorics

\begin{theorem}
In any coloring of the edges of a sufficiently large complete graph, one will find monochromatic complete subgraphs.
For 2 colors, Ramsey's theorem states that for any pair of positive integers $\tuple {r, s}$, there exists a least positive integer $\map R {r, s}$ such that for any complete graph on $\map R {r, s}$ vertices, whose edges are colored red or blue, there exists either a complete subgraph on $r$ vertices which is entirely red, or a complete subgraph on $s$ vertices which is entirely blue.
More generally, for any given number of colors $c$, and any given integers $n_1, \ldots, n_c$, there is a number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ such that:
:if the edges of a complete graph of order $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ are colored with $c$ different colours, then for some $i$ between $1$ and $c$, it must contain a complete subgraph of order $n_i$ whose edges are all color $i$.
This number $\map R {n_1, \ldots, n_c}$ is called the Ramsey number for $n_1, \ldots, n_c$.
The special case above has $c = 2$ (and $n_1 = r$ and $n_2 = s$).
Here $\map R {r, s}$ signifies an integer that depends on both $r$ and $s$. It is understood to represent the smallest integer for which the theorem holds.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
First we prove the theorem for the 2-color case, by induction on $r + s$.
It is clear from the definition that
:$\forall n \in \N: \map R {n, 1} = \map R {1, n} = 1$
because the complete graph on one node has no edges.
This is the base case.
We prove that $R \left({r, s}\right)$ exists by finding an explicit bound for it.
By the inductive hypothesis, $\map R {r - 1, s}$ and $\map R {r, s - 1}$ exist.
\end{proof}

Hmm, the user is asking about a party scenario where we need to ensure that either four people all know each other or four people are all complete strangers. This sounds familiar—it's a classic problem in combinatorics, specifically Ramsey theory. 

Looking at the retrieved documents, Document 4 and Document 5 discuss Ramsey numbers explicitly. Document 4 defines Ramsey numbers, explaining that for edge colorings of complete graphs, it guarantees monochromatic cliques. Document 5 gives Ramsey's theorem and a proof sketch for the two-color case, which matches the party problem (knowing/not knowing as two "colors"). 

The key here is that the user's question translates to finding the Ramsey number R(4,4). Document 5 mentions R(r,s) as the smallest integer where a red clique of size r or blue clique of size s is forced. For four mutual acquaintances or four mutual strangers, we need R(4,4). 

I recall that R(4,4) is 18, though the documents don't state the value. The proof in Document 5 shows R(r,s) exists inductively but doesn't compute specific numbers. Still, the framework is perfect: the party guests correspond to graph vertices, "knowing" is one edge color, "strangers" is the other. 

The other documents are irrelevant (group words, natural language, river crossing). I'll ignore them. The answer must be R(4,4)=18. I should explain the Ramsey theory connection briefly since the user might not know the terminology, but the documents provide enough context to justify it.

To ensure that in any group of guests, there are either four people who all know each other (a clique of size 4) or four people who are all mutual strangers (an independent set of size 4), you need **18 guests**. This is determined by the **Ramsey number \(R(4,4)\)**.

### Explanation:
- **Ramsey theory** addresses scenarios where a certain degree of order must appear in large structures. The Ramsey number \(R(r,s)\) is the smallest integer such that in any 2-coloring of the edges of a complete graph with that many vertices, there is either a monochromatic clique of size \(r\) (one color) or a clique of size \(s\) (the other color).
- Here, "knowing each other" and "being strangers" correspond to two edge colors in a complete graph:
  - **Edge color 1 (e.g., red)**: Two people know each other.
  - **Edge color 2 (e.g., blue)**: Two people are strangers.
- **\(R(4,4) = 18\)** means that with 18 guests, it is *guaranteed* that either:
  - Four people all know each other (a red clique of size 4), or
  - Four people are all mutual strangers (a blue clique of size 4).

### Why 18?
- While the retrieved documents define Ramsey numbers and Ramsey's theorem (Documents 4 and 5), they do not compute \(R(4,4)\) explicitly.
- However, it is a known result in combinatorics that \(R(4,4) = 18\). This has been verified through extensive combinatorial arguments and computations.
- Smaller groups (e.g., 17 guests) can avoid both configurations, proving 18 is the minimum required.

### Key Insight from Ramsey Theory:
Ramsey's theorem (Document 5) ensures that such a number exists, and for \(r = s = 4\), that number is 18. This applies directly to social scenarios like parties, where relationships are modeled as graph edges.

**Answer: You need 18 guests.**

The problem involves arranging the outcomes of 6 games on a bracket such that, for every game, the number of wins is never less than the number of losses at any point in the sequence of games. This condition translates to ensuring that in the sequence of game outcomes, where each outcome is recorded as a win (W) for one side or a loss (L) for the other (depending on the bracket's structure), the cumulative number of W's is always at least the cumulative number of L's after each game in the sequence.

The sequence of outcomes consists of 6 symbols, each being W or L. The condition requires that for any prefix of the sequence, the number of W's is greater than or equal to the number of L's. This is equivalent to a path in a random walk starting at 0, where each W increases the count by 1 and each L decreases it by 1, and the path must stay non-negative at all steps.

The number of such sequences for \(n\) games is given by the binomial coefficient \(\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}\). For \(n = 6\), this is \(\binom{6}{3}\):

\[
\binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3!(6-3)!} = \frac{6 \times 5 \times 4}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 20.
\]

Each such sequence can be realized with an appropriate bracket structure and order of games. For example, a right-branching tournament can be used, where the first game is between two teams, the winner plays the next team, and so on, with outcomes assigned according to the sequence (e.g., W if the left team wins, L if the right team wins). The sequence satisfies the condition by construction, and different sequences correspond to different arrangements of outcomes on the bracket.

Thus, there are 20 different ways to arrange the outcomes of the 6 games on the bracket satisfying the given condition.

\boxed{20}

question:
Alice has $1,000,000 and is considering betting $100,000 on a coin flip where she doubles her bet if she wins. Given that Alice evaluates her decisions based on how the outcome would affect her happiness on a logarithmic utility of weath function, what is the minimum chance of winning she must assign to the coin flip to justify her bet?

[Document 1]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Rational Decision-Maker]
Let $G$ be a game.
Let $P$ be a player of $G$.
Let $A$ be the set of moves available to $P$.
Let $C$ be the set $C$ of consequences of the moves available to $P$.
Let $g: A \to C$ be the consequence function on $A$.
Let $B \subseteq A$ be a the set of moves which are feasible.
$P$ is a '''rational decision-maker''' {{iff}} $P$ chooses an element $a^* \in B$ such that:
:$\forall a \in a^*: \map g {a^*} \succsim \map g a$
where $\succsim$ is the preference relation on $C$.
That is, that $P$ solves the problem:
:$\ds \max_{a \mathop \in B} \map U {\map g a}$
where $U$ is the utility function on $C$.
It is assumed that $P$ uses the same preference relation for all $B \subseteq A$.
\end{definition}

[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Ansatz]
An '''Ansatz''' is a guessed solution, or general form of a solution, to a mathematical problem.
A mathematician making the '''Ansatz''' will then attempt to prove that the '''Ansatz''' is indeed a solution to the original problem.
If he or she succeeds, the '''Ansatz''' is justified retroactively.
\end{definition}

[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Coin/Coin-Tossing]
'''Coin-tossing''' is a technique to select randomly one of two options. The usual scenario is to resolve an issue between two parties. The most usual of these is to determine which of the two parties is to choose whether to take the first move, or otherwise determine the starting arrangement, in a game or sport.
A coin is thrown into the air in such a manner that it turns over and over. This can be conveniently achieved by coiling the hand into a loose fist, balancing the coin on the first knuckle of the index finger, and then pinging the thumb up from under the index finger by a physical process derived from the science of catastrophe theory. The nail of the thumb will then impact smartly against the edge of the coin which projects over the edge of the index finger's knuckle, propelling it upwards in such a manner that, by an application of the physics of angular momentum, it will be caused to rotate more-or-less rapidly about a diameter.
When it descends, the side which will remain uppermost will be dependent upon both the speed of rotation and the distance travelled, neither of which is easy to gauge precisely enough to make the process predictable.
The descending coin will either be caught in the hand by the one flipping it (the "tosser"), to be immediately clasped to the back of the other hand, or allowed to fall directly to the ground. The other party (the "caller") is offered the chance of guessing which of the two sides of the coin is on top. The two sides are usually referred to (in the English-speaking world) as "heads" and "tails". The "heads" side tends to be the one that features the head of state of the nation to which the coin belongs, while the "tails" is the other side.
Once the caller has made the call, the uppermost side of the coin is revealed. If the side matches that called by the caller, the caller has won, and is given the option of taking the choice as to the starting configuration of the game. Otherwise the tosser has won, and takes the option.<ref>The play ''{{WP|Rosencrantz_and_Guildenstern_Are_Dead|Rosencrantz and Guildenstern are Dead}}'' by {{WP|Tom_Stoppard|Tom Stoppard}} begins with an unfeasibly long sequence of "heads" calls in a game of '''coin-tossing'''.</ref>
\end{definition}

[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:RSA Algorithm]
The '''RSA algorithm''' is a technique of encoding a message such that the method of encoding can be made public without compromising the security.
Let Alice be sending a message to Bob.
Alice and Bob both agree on two large primes $p$ and $q$, each having at least $100$ digits.
Let $M = p q$.
$M$ can be made public if they so wish.
Let $K = \left({p - 1}\right) \left({q - 1}\right)$.
$K$ is to be kept secret.
Let Alice choose some number $a$ such that $a \perp K$.
$a$ is made known to Bob, and may even be made public.
Alice represents her message in a series of numbers in the range $0$ to $M$.
Let $x$ be one such number.
Alice calculates $y \equiv x^a \pmod M$.
The sequence of numbers $y$ is sent to Bob as the coded message.
Bob needs to know a number $b$ such that $a b \equiv 1 \pmod K$.
This number needs to be kept secret.
To decode $y$, Bob computes $y^b \pmod M$.
This works because of Fermat's Little Theorem:
:$y^b \equiv \left({x^a}\right)^b \equiv x^1 = x \pmod M$
The method works because:
:$(1): \quad$ There are efficient tests to find large primes
:$(2): \quad$ There are no known methods for finding the prime factors of large numbers efficiently.
So making $p q$ public does not help $p$ and $q$ be found, and without those it is impossible to work out what $b$ is.
{{NamedforDef|Ronald Linn Rivest|name2 = Adi Shamir|name3 = Leonard Max Adleman|cat = Rivest|cat2 = Shamir|cat3 = Adleman}}
\end{definition}

[Document 5]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Astrolabe/Historical Note]
The '''astrolabe''' dates to the time of {{AuthorRef|Apollonius of Perga}}, although some sources credit it invention to {{AuthorRef|Hipparchus of Nicaea}}.
{{AuthorRef|Theon of Alexandria}} wrote a detailed treatise on the '''astrolabe'''.
It is supposed that {{AuthorRef|Ptolemy}} may have used an '''astrolabe''' to provide the data for his {{BookLink|Apotelesmatika|Ptolemy}}.
Some authors attribute the invention of the '''astrolabe''' to {{AuthorRef|Hypatia of Alexandria}}, based on a misinterpretation of a letter written by one of her students, but it is well established that it had been invented some $500$ years earlier.
Use of the '''astrolabe''' was adopted by the medieval Arab world, where its design was made considerably more sophisticated and intricate.
\end{definition}

Alice has a wealth of $1,000,000 and is considering betting $100,000 on a coin flip where she doubles her bet if she wins, resulting in a net gain of $100,000, or loses her bet if she loses, resulting in a net loss of $100,000. Alice uses a logarithmic utility function, \(U(w) = \ln(w)\), to evaluate her decisions based on how outcomes affect her happiness.

To justify taking the bet, the expected utility of betting must be at least equal to the utility of not betting. Let \(w = 1,000,000\) be her current wealth and \(b = 100,000\) be the bet amount.

- If she does not bet, her utility is \(\ln(w) = \ln(1,000,000)\).
- If she bets, with probability \(p\) of winning, her wealth is \(w + b = 1,100,000\), so utility is \(\ln(1,100,000)\).
- With probability \(1 - p\) of losing, her wealth is \(w - b = 900,000\), so utility is \(\ln(900,000)\).

The expected utility of betting is:
\[
E[U] = p \cdot \ln(1,100,000) + (1 - p) \cdot \ln(900,000)
\]

Set this equal to the utility of not betting for indifference:
\[
p \cdot \ln(1,100,000) + (1 - p) \cdot \ln(900,000) = \ln(1,000,000)
\]

Solving for \(p\):
\[
p \cdot \ln(1,100,000) + \ln(900,000) - p \cdot \ln(900,000) = \ln(1,000,000)
\]
\[
p \cdot (\ln(1,100,000) - \ln(900,000)) = \ln(1,000,000) - \ln(900,000)
\]
\[
p = \frac{\ln(1,000,000) - \ln(900,000)}{\ln(1,100,000) - \ln(900,000)} = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{1,000,000}{900,000}\right)}{\ln\left(\frac{1,100,000}{900,000}\right)} = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{10}{9}\right)}{\ln\left(\frac{11}{9}\right)}
\]

Numerically:
- \(\ln(10/9) \approx 0.1053605156578263\)
- \(\ln(11/9) \approx 0.2006706954621511\)
- \(p \approx 0.1053605156578263 / 0.2006706954621511 \approx 0.525023\)

Thus, \(p \approx 0.5250\) (rounded to four decimal places).

For Alice to justify the bet, the expected utility must be at least equal to the utility of not betting, which occurs at \(p \approx 0.5250\). Therefore, she must assign a minimum probability of winning of approximately 0.525 to make the bet acceptable.

The retrieved documents provide context but are not directly needed for the calculation. Document 1 confirms that Alice, as a rational decision-maker, maximizes expected utility, which is applied here. The other documents are not relevant to the utility calculation.

The series in question is defined by the general term \(a_n = \frac{n-1}{n^3 + 1}\), starting from iteration \(n = 0\). The first few terms are:
- \(n = 0\): \(\frac{0-1}{0^3 + 1} = -1\)
- \(n = 1\): \(\frac{1-1}{1^3 + 1} = 0\)
- \(n = 2\): \(\frac{2-1}{8 + 1} = \frac{1}{9}\)
- \(n = 3\): \(\frac{3-1}{27 + 1} = \frac{2}{28} = \frac{1}{14}\)

The series is \(S = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\). Since \(a_0 = -1\) and \(a_1 = 0\), the convergence of \(S\) depends on \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} a_n\).

### Convergence Analysis
To determine convergence, apply the **Limit Comparison Test** (Document 3) with the comparison series \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} b_n\), where \(b_n = \frac{1}{n^2}\). This choice is motivated by the asymptotic behavior of \(a_n\):
\[
a_n = \frac{n-1}{n^3 + 1} \sim \frac{n}{n^3} = \frac{1}{n^2} \quad \text{as} \quad n \to \infty.
\]

Compute the limit:
\[
L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{(n-1)/(n^3 + 1)}{1/n^2} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{(n-1) \cdot n^2}{n^3 + 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^3 - n^2}{n^3 + 1}.
\]
Dividing numerator and denominator by \(n^3\):
\[
L = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1 - \frac{1}{n}}{1 + \frac{1}{n^3}} = \frac{1 - 0}{1 + 0} = 1.
\]
Since \(L = 1 > 0\) (finite and positive), the Limit Comparison Test implies that \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} a_n\) and \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} b_n\) share the same convergence behavior.

The series \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} b_n = \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2}\) is a \(p\)-series with \(p = 2 > 1\). By the **Convergence of \(p\)-Series** (Document 4), \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2}\) converges. Thus, \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} a_n\) also converges.

### Conclusion
The series \(S = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n\) converges because:
- The \(n=0\) term (\(-1\)) is a constant.
- The \(n=1\) term is \(0\).
- The tail \(\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} a_n\) converges.

**Implication for the digital image:** Since the series converges, the total "clarity" approaches a fixed limit. After a sufficiently large number of pieces, adding more terms will not significantly change the image. The remaining terms form a convergent tail, and their contribution becomes arbitrarily small (smaller than any given threshold).

Thus, **there will come a point when adding more pieces doesn't significantly change the image**. The series does not require infinitely many terms to achieve a stable representation.

question:
Imagine you're organizing a small local sports league with 9 players and decide to create 3 teams, each team having exactly 3 players. Assuming each team is equal in status and the only difference is the roster, how many different ways can you form these teams?

\section{Multinomial Theorem}
Tags: Multinomial Coefficients, Binomial Coefficients, Discrete Mathematics, Proofs by Induction, Algebra

\begin{theorem}
Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k \in F$, where $F$ is a field.
Then:
:$\ds \paren {x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_m}^n = \sum_{k_1 \mathop + k_2 \mathop + \mathop \cdots \mathop + k_m \mathop = n} \binom n {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m} {x_1}^{k_1} {x_2}^{k_2} \cdots {x_m}^{k_m}$
where:
:$m \in \Z_{> 0}$ is a positive integer
:$n \in \Z_{\ge 0}$ is a non-negative integer
:$\dbinom n {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m} = \dfrac {n!} {k_1! \, k_2! \, \cdots k_m!}$ denotes a multinomial coefficient.
The sum is taken for all non-negative integers $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m$ such that $k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m = n$, and with the understanding that wherever $0^0$ may appear  it shall be considered to have a value of $1$.
The '''multinomial theorem''' is a generalization of the Binomial Theorem.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The proof proceeds by induction on $m$.
For each $m \in \N_{\ge 1}$, let $\map P m$ be the proposition:
:$\ds \forall n \in \N: \paren {x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_m}^n = \sum_{k_1 \mathop + k_2 \mathop + \mathop \cdots \mathop + k_m \mathop = n} \binom n {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m} {x_1}^{k_1} {x_2}^{k_2} \cdots {x_m}^{k_m}$
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Bozo Bit]
The concept of '''bozo bit''' was adopted by the computer industry in the following manner.
The typical stereotype of a worker in the computer software industry is that he (usually male) is limited in his ability to interact skilfully with his colleagues.
In a team of people, there are bound to be clashes of personality. During the course of interactions with one of your colleagues, one of them sticks in your mind for making unhelpful suggestions, or crafting inept designs, or otherwise demonstrating a modicum of stupidity that is likely to become a hindrance to the task in progress.
What can happen is for a team leader, or someone else whose input is influential, to register this person as a bozo, and prejudge every contribution of that person to be worthless. When the bozo is speaking, the listener mentally tunes out the sound of his voice and is instead thinking of something completely different.
Such behaviour is known as '''flipping the bozo bit''' on that person.
\end{definition}

\section{1089 Trick}
Tags: Reversals, Classic Problems, Interesting Numbers, Think of a Number Puzzles, Specific Numbers, 1089

\begin{theorem}
Take a three-digit number (one where the first and last digits are different by at least $2$).
Reverse it, and get the difference between that and the first number.
Reverse that, and add that to the difference you calculated just now.

\section{Multinomial Coefficient expressed as Product of Binomial Coefficients}
Tags: Multinomial Coefficients, Binomial Coefficients

\begin{theorem}
:$\dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m} {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m} = \dbinom {k_1 + k_2} {k_1} \dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + k_3} {k_1 + k_2} \cdots \dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m} {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_{m - 1} }$
where:
:$\dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m} {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m}$ denotes a multinomial coefficient
:$\dbinom {k_1 + k_2} {k_1}$ etc. denotes binomial coefficients.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The proof proceeds by induction.
For all $m \in \Z_{> 1}$, let $\map P m$ be the proposition:
:$\dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m} {k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_m} = \dbinom {k_1 + k_2} {k_1} \dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + k_3} {k_1 + k_2} \cdots \dbinom {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_m} {k_1 + k_2 + \cdots + k_{m - 1} }$
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Set/Uniqueness of Elements/Multiple Specification]
For a given '''set''', an object is either '''in''' the set or '''not in''' the set.
So, if an element is in a set, then it is in the set '''only once''', however many times it may appear in the definition of the set.
Thus, the set $\set {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4}$ is the same set as $\set {1, 2, 3, 4}$.
$2$ and $3$ are in the set, and '''listing''' them twice makes no difference to the set's '''contents'''.
Like the membership of a club, if you're in, you're in -- however many membership cards you have to prove it.
\end{definition}

To determine the number of different ways to form three teams of three players each from nine distinct players, where the teams are indistinct (i.e., the order of the teams does not matter) and the order of players within each team does not matter, the calculation involves combinatorial methods accounting for these symmetries.

### Step-by-Step Calculation:
1. **Choose 3 players out of 9 for the first team (if teams were distinct):**  
   The number of ways to choose 3 players from 9 is given by the binomial coefficient:  
   \[
   \binom{9}{3} = \frac{9!}{3!(9-3)!} = \frac{9!}{3! \cdot 6!} = 84
   \]

2. **Choose 3 players out of the remaining 6 for the second team (if teams were distinct):**  
   \[
   \binom{6}{3} = \frac{6!}{3! \cdot 3!} = 20
   \]

3. **The last 3 players form the third team:**  
   \[
   \binom{3}{3} = 1
   \]

4. **Total ways if teams were distinct (ordered teams):**  
   Multiplying the above gives the number of ways to assign players to three distinct teams:  
   \[
   84 \times 20 \times 1 = 1680
   \]

5. **Account for indistinct teams:**  
   Since the teams are indistinct (i.e., the order of the teams does not matter), each unique grouping of players into teams is counted multiple times in the 1680 figure—specifically, once for each permutation of the three teams. The number of ways to permute three teams is \(3! = 6\).  
   Therefore, divide by 6 to correct for overcounting:  
   \[
   \frac{1680}{6} = 280
   \]

### Alternative Method Using Multinomial Coefficients:
- The multinomial coefficient \(\binom{9}{3,3,3}\) gives the number of ways to divide 9 distinct players into three distinct teams of three players each:  
  \[
  \binom{9}{3,3,3} = \frac{9!}{3! \cdot 3! \cdot 3!} = \frac{362880}{6 \times 6 \times 6} = \frac{362880}{216} = 1680
  \]
- Since the teams are indistinct, divide by \(3! = 6\):  
  \[
  \frac{1680}{6} = 280
  \]

### Verification with Smaller Case:
Consider 4 players divided into 2 indistinct teams of 2:
- \(\binom{4}{2} = 6\) ways to choose the first team (if teams were distinct), but since teams are indistinct, divide by \(2! = 2\):  
  \[
  \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ unique groupings}
  \]
- Explicitly: Players A, B, C, D. The groupings are:  
  {A,B} and {C,D}; {A,C} and {B,D}; {A,D} and {B,C}. This confirms 3 ways.

### Conclusion:
The number of different ways to form three indistinct teams of three players each from nine players is 280. This accounts for the indistinct nature of the teams and the fact that only the roster (i.e., the composition of the teams) matters.

\boxed{280}

[Document 1]
\section{Number of Hamilton Cycles in Complete Graph}
Tags: Hamiltonian Graphs, Graph Theory, Combinatorics

\begin{theorem}
For all $n \ge 3$, the number of distinct Hamilton cycles in the complete graph $K_n$ is $\dfrac {\left({n-1}\right)!} 2$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
In a complete graph, every vertex is adjacent to every other vertex.
Therefore, if we were to take all the vertices in a complete graph in any order, there will be a path through those vertices in that order.
Joining either end of that path gives us a Hamilton cycle.
From Cardinality of Set of Bijections, there are $n!$ different ways of picking the vertices of $G$ in some order.
Hence there are $n!$ ways of building such a Hamilton cycle.
Not all these are different, though.
On any such cycle, there are:
:$n$ different places you can start
:$2$ different directions you can travel.
So any one of these $n!$ cycles is in a set of $2 n$ cycles which all contain the same set of edges.
So there are $\dfrac {n!} {2 n} = \dfrac {\paren {n - 1}!} 2$ distinct Hamilton cycles.
{{qed}}
Category:Hamiltonian Graphs
Category:Combinatorics
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Definition:Stirling Numbers of the Second Kind/Definition 1]
'''Stirling numbers of the second kind''' are defined recursively by:
:$\ds {n \brace k} := \begin{cases}
\delta_{n k} & : k = 0 \text{ or } n = 0 \\
& \\
\ds {n - 1 \brace k - 1} + k {n - 1 \brace k} & : \text{otherwise} \\
\end{cases}$
where:
: $\delta_{n k}$ is the Kronecker delta
: $n$ and $k$ are non-negative integers.
\end{definition}
